advantages: Threshold-independent — compare binary classifiers before choosing a deployment cutoff tied to business costs.
Summarizes ranking quality in one scalar (AUC) while roc_curve retains full TPR/FPR trade-off detail.
Works with any real-valued score (probabilities, logits, SVM margins) as long as higher means more likely positive.
Scale-invariant to strictly monotonic transforms of scores — only the order matters, not calibration.
Standard and widely supported in libraries (sklearn, PyTorch metrics, MLflow) for reproducible benchmarking.
disadvantages: Does not measure calibration — high AUC allows poorly calibrated probabilities; use Brier score or reliability diagrams if ŷ must be trusted as probability.
Can be optimistic on highly imbalanced data when negatives dominate — FPR denominator is huge so many false positives look small.
Hides the operating point — does not tell you precision or FPR at the TPR your application requires.
Defined for binary (or one-vs-rest) tasks — not a direct summary metric for multi-label or multiclass without reduction.
Less informative than PR-AUC when positives are very rare and you care about positive-class precision at workable recall.
